questions.to_find: Which interchange of semaphore operations in the producer/consumer processes can cause a deadlock

understand.plain: This problem involves a bounded buffer shared between a producer and a consumer, synchronized using three semaphores: S (mutex), F (free slots), and E (filled slots). We need to check which re-ordering of Wait/Signal calls can cause a circular wait (deadlock). A deadlock happens when the producer and consumer each hold a semaphore that the other needs.

keywords.explain: A shared buffer of fixed size N used between producer and consumer processes
keywords.example: A buffer with 5 slots: producer fills, consumer empties

keywords.explain: A synchronization variable; Wait(sem) decrements it (blocks if 0), Signal(sem) increments it (wakes a blocked process)
keywords.example: S=1 acts as a mutex; Wait(S) acquires lock, Signal(S) releases it

keywords.explain: A state where two or more processes are blocked indefinitely, each waiting for a resource held by the other
keywords.example: Producer holds S and waits for F; Consumer holds nothing waiting for E — stalemate

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing modified Producer (Wait(S) before Wait(F)) and Consumer. When buffer is full: Producer holds S and waits on F=0. Consumer is blocked on E waiting for something to be appended, but Producer holds S so buffer cannot be updated — circular wait (deadlock).

given.aim: Determine which semaphore operation swap causes deadlock in the bounded buffer problem

terms.meaning: Ensures mutual exclusion for accessing the shared buffer
terms.example: Only one process (producer or consumer) can access the buffer at a time
terms.connects: Swapping S with F in Producer can lead to holding S while waiting on F

terms.meaning: Counts the number of free slots available in the buffer
terms.example: Starts at N; decremented by Producer when it adds an item
terms.connects: When buffer is full, F=0 and Producer blocks on it

terms.meaning: Counts the number of filled slots in the buffer
terms.example: Starts at 0; incremented by Producer after appending
terms.connects: Consumer blocks on E when buffer is empty, preventing it from freeing F

given.plan: Simulate each swap: (I) In Producer, if Wait(S) comes before Wait(F), the Producer acquires S then waits on F. If buffer is full, F=0 so Producer blocks while holding S. Consumer needs S to remove an item and signal F, but it cannot acquire S — deadlock. (II) Swapping Signal(S) and Signal(F) in Consumer only changes order of releasing signals (both eventually released) — no deadlock.

steps.title: Analyze interchange I: swap Wait(F) and Wait(S) in Producer
steps.formula_raw: Deadlock condition: circular wait on resources
steps.apply: Original Producer: Wait(F) then Wait(S). Modified: Wait(S) then Wait(F). Scenario: Suppose buffer is completely full (F=0). Producer executes Wait(S) — succeeds (S becomes 0, Producer holds mutex). Producer then executes Wait(F) — F=0, so Producer BLOCKS while holding S. Now Consumer tries to run: Wait(E) succeeds (E>0), then Wait(S) — but S=0 (held by Producer) so Consumer BLOCKS. Neither can proceed: Producer waits for Consumer to free a slot (needs S), Consumer waits for S (held by Producer). DEADLOCK.
steps.note: The key is: holding the mutex (S) while waiting for a resource (F) that only the other process can free — classic deadlock pattern.

steps.title: Analyze interchange II: swap Signal(S) and Signal(F) in Consumer
steps.formula_raw: Signal operations are commutative for correctness
steps.apply: Original Consumer: Signal(S) then Signal(F). Modified: Signal(F) then Signal(S). In both orderings, the Consumer releases both semaphores. The order of releasing does not cause a deadlock — the Producer is not holding any resource that it needs to give up to receive S or F. Both signals are eventually executed, so no circular wait occurs.
steps.note: Swapping Signal operations is generally safe because releasing resources in different orders does not create circular waits — it may affect scheduling but not correctness/deadlock.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Only interchange I (swapping Wait(F) and Wait(S) in the Producer) can cause a deadlock. Interchange II (swapping Signal(S) and Signal(F) in the Consumer) does not cause a deadlock.
steps.note: The general rule: avoid acquiring a mutex BEFORE a resource semaphore when the resource may be unavailable. Always acquire resource semaphores before the mutex.

solution.result: Only interchange (i) causes a deadlock. Answer: A. (i) only

questions.formula_note: Deadlock from nested Wait: holding a mutex while blocking on a resource semaphore creates circular wait. The standard rule is to always Wait on resource semaphores (F or E) before the mutex (S).

questions.answer: A. (i) is true and (ii) is false
questions.to_find: Whether the given mutual exclusion attempt can allow concurrent access to critical region and/or cause deadlock

understand.plain: Two processes P1 and P2 try to use a shared boolean flag (critical_flag) to implement mutual exclusion. The flag is checked and then set in two separate steps (not atomically). We need to decide if both can enter the critical region at the same time (race condition) and if a deadlock is possible.

keywords.explain: Only one process at a time should be in the critical region
keywords.example: If P1 is in critical_region(), P2 must wait

keywords.explain: A bug where the outcome depends on the relative timing of two processes' operations on shared data
keywords.example: Both P1 and P2 read critical_flag==FALSE before either sets it to TRUE

keywords.explain: An operation that completes entirely without interruption; test-and-set must be atomic for mutual exclusion to work
keywords.example: Hardware test-and-set: reads and writes the flag in one uninterruptible instruction

keywords.explain: A state where processes wait for each other indefinitely, none able to proceed
keywords.example: P1 holds lock A and waits for lock B; P2 holds lock B and waits for lock A

YES</text><rect x='300' y='90' width='220' height='28' rx='4' class='no'/><text x='410' y='109' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>critical_flag = TRUE</text><text x='410' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>critical_region()</text><rect x='300' y='152' width='220' height='36' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='410' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>Both P1 and P2 enter</text><text x='410' y='184' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>critical_region! Race!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing P1 reads critical_flag==FALSE, then a context switch occurs before P1 sets the flag. P2 runs, also reads critical_flag==FALSE, sets it TRUE, enters critical_region. Then P1 resumes, sets critical_flag=TRUE, and also enters critical_region — both processes are in the critical region simultaneously (race condition, not deadlock).

given.aim: Determine if the flag-based mutual exclusion allows concurrent critical region access and/or deadlock

terms.meaning: Shared variable used as a lock; FALSE = available, TRUE = in use
terms.example: Both processes check and set this flag, but not atomically
terms.connects: The non-atomic check-then-set is the source of the race condition

terms.meaning: The check (if flag==FALSE) and set (flag=TRUE) are two separate instructions, allowing a context switch in between
terms.example: P1 reads FALSE, context switch, P2 reads FALSE and sets TRUE, P1 resumes and sets TRUE — both proceed
terms.connects: Directly causes statement (i) to be TRUE

terms.meaning: Deadlock = processes block waiting for each other. Race condition = processes proceed without proper synchronization (both enter critical region)
terms.example: This code has a race condition, NOT a deadlock — no process waits forever
terms.connects: Statement (ii) is FALSE because no circular waiting occurs here

given.plan: Check for race: if both P1 and P2 read critical_flag==FALSE before either sets it TRUE (possible with context switch between read and write), both enter critical_region simultaneously — so (i) is TRUE. Check for deadlock: deadlock requires circular waiting on resources. Here, if critical_flag is FALSE, any process can enter; if TRUE, a process simply skips (does not enter and does not wait). No process ever blocks waiting for another — so no deadlock. Statement (ii) is FALSE.

steps.title: Check statement (i): Can both P1 and P2 access critical_region concurrently?
steps.formula_raw: Race condition: check(flag) and set(flag) are non-atomic
steps.apply:
Execution sequence that leads to concurrent access:
1. P1 executes: if (critical_flag == FALSE) → TRUE (flag is FALSE), condition holds
2. Context switch to P2 before P1 executes critical_flag = TRUE
3. P2 executes: if (critical_flag == FALSE) → TRUE (flag still FALSE, P1 hasn't set it yet)
4. P2 executes: critical_flag = TRUE
5. P2 enters and executes critical_region()
6. Context switch back to P1
7. P1 executes: critical_flag = TRUE (already TRUE, no re-check)
8. P1 enters and executes critical_region()
Both P1 and P2 are in critical_region at the same time. Statement (i) is TRUE.
steps.note: The fundamental flaw: the if-condition check and the flag assignment are two separate non-atomic operations. A context switch between them breaks mutual exclusion.

steps.title: Check statement (ii): Does this lead to deadlock?
steps.formula_raw: Deadlock requires: mutual exclusion + hold & wait + no preemption + circular wait
steps.apply: In this code, if critical_flag == TRUE, the process simply does NOT enter the if-block — it returns from get_exclusive_access() without waiting. No process is ever blocked spinning or sleeping waiting for another process to release the flag. There is no circular wait. Therefore, no deadlock can occur. Statement (ii) is FALSE.
steps.note: If the code had a busy-wait loop (while(critical_flag == TRUE) {}) instead of the if-condition, we'd need to analyze further for livelock. But even then, deadlock (as defined in OS) requires circular waiting on non-preemptible resources — not just a liveness issue.

steps.title: Match to answer options
steps.apply: Statement (i) is TRUE. Statement (ii) is FALSE. This matches option A: '(i) is true and (ii) is false'.
steps.note: This is a classic example of why Peterson's algorithm or hardware atomic test-and-set is needed for correct mutual exclusion — a simple shared boolean flag without atomicity is insufficient.

solution.result: Statement (i) is true (both can access critical_region concurrently due to race condition) and statement (ii) is false (no deadlock occurs). Answer: A. (i) is true and (ii) is false

questions.formula_note: Non-atomic check-then-set of a shared flag violates the atomicity requirement for mutual exclusion. A context switch between the read and write of the flag allows two processes to enter the critical region simultaneously (race condition), but since no process blocks waiting for another, there is no deadlock.
